title: Treasure Hunt Engine: From Default Config to Production Ready — A Production Operator Guide
author: Veltrix
excerpt: A practical breakdown of the Veltrix configuration layer that determines whether your server scales cleanly or stalls at the first growth inflection point.

content.text: Operators often discover issues with Veltrix only after the Treasure Hunt Engine has begun live. When a Hytale server launches, the assumption is that a static events.yml file is enough. In reality, Veltrix's event scheduling relies on a precise hierarchy of timestamps, coordinates, and reward hierarchies that are rarely documented in a single place. A common stumbling block appears when the Treasure Hunt Engine is told to infer start times from a DateTime string that does not match the server’s locale, causing the engine to start tasks days later or before the server is ready. The production problem is primarily the fragile mapping between YAML structures and real‑time execution: a missing “location” entry or an improperly nested “prize” block can make the entire hunt invisible to players, but the error only surfaces once players begin loot chases and the logs show a cascade of errors. This latent bug compels operators to hunt for configuration tips once the hunt is live, not during the pre‑launch phase.
What Operators Are Actually Searching For

The pattern of queries that surface after deployment shows operators are tripping over the same set of committed values across events.yml, prizes.yml, announce.yml, and tournament.yml. For instance, a recurring question is why the Treasure Hunt Engine stops after the first prize claim, pointing to a missing “repeat” flag in the prize definition. Operators also find themselves debugging broadcast timing – as the announce.yml expects a 12:00 UTC format, yet the server pushes messages at 12:30 local time, causing confusion about when players receive the event header. Many turn to the bracket configuration in tournament.yml because the engine interprets the seed numbering incorrectly, so matchups are accidentally reversed when the schedule is read after midnight. These stumbling points reveal that operators are often dealing with form validation gaps: they fill out the YAML files with the right logic, but syntactic nuances such as a trailing comma or mismatched indentation turn a fully working engine into a silent failure that only surfaces during live play.
Event Architecture and Why Structure Determines Participation

The overall architecture of a Hytale event in Veltrix dictates how players decide to join, compete, or simply observe. In events.yml, two parameters—maxParticipants and playerLimitMode—control the size of each event instance and whether a queue is used or an instant drop‑in mode. Coupled with prizes.yml’s rewardTier configuration, players see clear expectations: a higher tier reward triggers a competitive mindset while a fixed small prize encourages casual play. The requiredLevel field sets a skill gate; if set too high, new players are blocked, shrinking the active pool; if too low, high‑level players may feel the event is unchallenging. Additionally, announce.yml’s broadcastEnabled flag and its broadcastInterval shape visibility. A short interval can spam players and reduce anticipation, whereas a long interval may make the event feel forgotten. Together, these structural settings map directly onto the engagement curve: precise participant limits attract balanced competition, tiered rewards sustain repetition, and smart announcements build excitement at the right moments.
Schedule Design: The Rhythm That Creates Login Habits

A well‑designed rhythm keeps players logging in for the next round. In events.yml the startTime and duration parameters set when a session begins and how long it runs; recurrence schedules (e.g., weekly, daily) control refresh rate. Misusing startTime by referencing the wrong timezone or forgetting to apply daylight‑savings transformations causes the event to launch at odd player hours, swallowing away early participation. In announce.yml, broadcastInterval denotes how soon the system posts a reminder; setting it to a few seconds underestimates conversational readiness and ends up as a spam trigger, while a too‑long interval might mean the event’s actual start time passes without alert. If operators neglect to enable launch notifications, players miss the cue entirely. Both misconfigurations break the predictable pattern that players rely on to plan their play sessions, turning a potential staple event into a “one‑off” gone unnoticed.

To maintain an active login habit with Veltrix, align event start times with cooldowns typical of zoning events and keep prize distributions transparent in prizes.yml: clear tiers encourage players to aim for higher rewards in successive runs.
The Mistakes That Compound Over Time

One common configuration blunder involves the timeformat key in events.yml. Operators who mistakenly set timeformat to a world time zone that does not match the server’s actual clock cause starttime and endtime to shift hours later or earlier. Players arrive to find the event already over or still in the waiting room, leading to frustration and repeat complaints. Another frequent mistake is hard‑coding the maxplayers value, such as setting maxplayers: 10 for a Treasure Hunt Engine that routinely attracts dozens of participants. When the numeric limit is reached, new players are dropped into a withheld queue that never clears, and the event stalls.
Implementation: The Correct Sequence

A third error occurs when the prizeitem field in prizes.yml is left empty or populated with a negative item ID. Because the engine cannot resolve the reward, prizes are distributed as zeros or dummy items and the sale value of the event diminishes. Finally, misconfiguring the announce.yml broadcastdelay parameter, for instance using 1 instead of 60 seconds, floods the chat with continuous notifications, disrupting gameplay and confusing participants.
The Parameters That Actually Control Behavior
Post title: Treasure Hunt Engine: From Default Config to Production Ready — A Production Operator Guide. Category: Events. Write THREE items separated by blank lines.

Key parameters in events.yml that influence how the Treasure Hunt Engine runs on a Veltrix‑hosted Hytale server are, in order of operational impact, event_duration, max_players, spawn_interval, treasure_size, treasure_chance, and notification_delay. For a small private house of 10–12 players, event_duration should be set to 20 minutes to maintain engagement, while max_players is 10. When the server hosts a public cohort of 200–300 players, increase event_duration to 35 minutes to allow exploration; bump max_players to the higher limit 200 and adjust spawn_interval to 30 seconds so treasures do not cluster. Treasure_size dictates the value of each chest; a moderate 8 chest per event works for 50–100 players, but for bigger audiences double the treasure_size to 16 to stretch the give‑away. Treasure_chance controls the probability any random location contains a chest; set 0.15 for 10–50 users, but raise to 0.25 for 200+ participants to offset higher spawn density. Finally, notification_delay moderates the broadcast lag; keep it at 5 seconds for small groups, but 10‑15 seconds for larger populations to avoid flooding logs.
When Your Server Starts to Scale

Key parameters in events.yml that influence how the Treasure Hunt Engine runs on a Veltrix‑hosted Hytale server are, in order of operational impact, event_duration, max_players, spawn_interval, treasure_size, treasure_chance, and notification_delay. For a small private house of 10–12 players, event_duration should be set to 20 minutes to maintain engagement, while max_players is 10. When the server hosts a public cohort of 200–300 players, increase event_duration to 35 minutes to allow exploration; bump max_players to the higher limit 200 and adjust spawn_interval to 30 seconds so treasures do not cluster. Treasure_size dictates the value of each chest; a moderate 8 chest per event works for 50–100 users, but for larger audiences double the treasure_size to 16 to stretch the give‑away. Treasure_chance controls the probability any random location contains a chest; set 0.15 for 10–50 users, but raise to 0.25 for 200+ participants to offset higher spawn density. Finally, notification_delay moderates the broadcast lag; keep it at 5 seconds for small groups, but 10–15 seconds for larger populations to avoid flooding logs.
Operator Checklist Before You Go Live

Before launch, the operator must confirm that events.yml defines every event type, that each event entry contains a schedule key with valid datetime strings, that the event's enabled flag is set to true, that no overlapping schedules occur, that all prize references in events.yml match identifiers defined in prizes.yml, that prizes.yml entries include type, amount, and a linked item in the item database, that announce.yml contains a broadcast key for each event tied to a fade-in and fade-out duration, that its interval key conforms to the server tick cycle, and that the announce.yml timings coincide with event start times.

Additionally, the operator must verify that tournament.yml is present for the Capture the Flag and Raid events, that bracket_difficulty aligns with the event difficulty level, that each bracket seed refers to a valid player group, and that no duplicate seed keys exist across the YAML file. The configuration files must pass a YAML syntax validator, load without errors into Veltrix, and the server must register all event names during startup. Finally, a dry run test must run the first event in a controlled environment with the event engine processing all hook calls successfully without runtime errors.
The Veltrix Treasure Hunt Engine handles this layer of your infrastructure automatically. Pre-configured, documented, and ready to deploy on your server.
